Students should understand both perspectives, recognize that interpretive method affects outcomes on issues like privacy, gun rights, and executive power, and evaluate the strengths and weaknesses of each approach. Vocabulary: originalism, textualism, living constitution, interpretation, framers' intent, judicial philosophy, strict construction, loose construction, precedent.

This topic covers the Evolution of the Constitution for Constitution 101 students (Const 101: Constitutional Amendments 11-27). Students should understand how the Constitution has been formally changed through amendments beyond the Bill of Rights. Key concepts: the Constitution has been amended only 27 times, reflecting the deliberate difficulty of the amendment process; key amendments include the 12th (revised Electoral College procedures), 13th-15th (Reconstruction Amendments abolishing slavery and extending rights), 16th (income tax), 17th (direct election of senators), 18th (Prohibition) and 21st (repeal of Prohibition, the only amendment repealing another), 19th (women's suffrage), 22nd (presidential term limits), 24th (abolishing poll taxes), 25th (presidential succession and disability), and 26th (18-year-old vote). Students should understand how amendments reflect changing American values, how some expanded democratic participation while others addressed structural issues, and why most proposed amendments fail. Vocabulary: amendment, ratification, Reconstruction Amendments, suffrage, Prohibition, term limits, presidential succession, poll tax, direct election.

This topic covers Landmark Supreme Court Cases for Constitution 101 students (Const 101: Judicial Review / Article III). Students should understand the most significant Supreme Court decisions that have shaped constitutional law. Key concepts: Marbury v. Madison (1803, established judicial review); McCulloch v. Maryland (1819, implied powers, national supremacy); Dred Scott v. Sandford (1857, denied citizenship to African Americans, struck down as one of the worst decisions); Plessy v. Ferguson (1896, 'separate but equal'); Brown v. Board of Education (1954, desegregation); Miranda v. Arizona (1966, rights of the accused); Roe v. Wade (1973, privacy/abortion rights, later overturned by Dobbs); Citizens United v. FEC (2010, corporate political speech). Students should understand how the Court interprets the Constitution through cases, that decisions can be overturned by later Courts or constitutional amendments, and that landmark cases often reflect broader social conflicts. Vocabulary: landmark case, precedent, stare decisis, majority opinion, dissent, concurrence, overturn, constitutional law, judicial review.

This topic covers Constitutional Debates for Constitution 101 students (Const 101: Federalist vs. Anti-Federalist Papers). Students should understand the foundational arguments for and against ratifying the Constitution. Key concepts: Federalists (Hamilton, Madison, Jay) supported ratification and wrote the Federalist Papers to persuade skeptics; Federalist No. 10 (Madison) argued that a large republic would control the dangers of factions; Federalist No. 51 (Madison) explained how separation of powers and checks and balances prevent tyranny; Federalist No. 70 (Hamilton) argued for a strong, energetic executive; Anti-Federalists (writing under pseudonyms including Brutus and Federal Farmer) warned that the Constitution created too powerful a central government, threatened state sovereignty, and lacked a bill of rights; Brutus No. 1 argued the nation was too large for a republic. Students should analyze both perspectives and understand how this debate led to the Bill of Rights as a compromise. Vocabulary: Federalist, Anti-Federalist, ratification debate, faction, republic, Federalist Papers, Brutus, tyranny, bill of rights, compromise.

This topic covers the Role of the Judiciary for Constitution 101 students (Const 101: Article III). Students should understand the structure, powers, and significance of the federal court system. Key concepts: Article III establishes the Supreme Court and authorizes Congress to create lower federal courts; federal judges serve 'during good behavior' (effectively lifetime tenure), insulating them from political pressure; the federal court system includes district courts (trial courts), courts of appeals (circuit courts), and the Supreme Court; the Supreme Court has both original jurisdiction (rare cases involving states or ambassadors) and appellate jurisdiction (most cases, reviewing lower court decisions); the Court chooses which cases to hear through writs of certiorari (Rule of Four); courts interpret laws, resolve disputes, and protect constitutional rights. Students should analyze debates about the appropriate role of courts in a democracy: should they defer to elected branches or actively protect rights? Vocabulary: Article III, federal judiciary, Supreme Court, district court, appellate court, jurisdiction, certiorari, lifetime tenure, independence, interpretation.

This topic covers the Legislative Process for Constitution 101 students (Const 101: Article I). Students should understand how Congress creates law and the structural features that shape legislative outcomes. Key concepts: Article I grants all legislative powers to Congress; a bill can be introduced in either chamber (except revenue bills, which must originate in the House); the committee system (standing committees, subcommittees) reviews, amends, and decides which bills advance; floor debate and voting procedures differ between House (Rules Committee controls debate) and Senate (unlimited debate, filibuster, cloture requiring 60 votes); conference committees reconcile differences between House and Senate versions; the president can sign or veto a bill; Congress can override a veto with two-thirds vote in both chambers. Students should understand why most bills die in committee, how the process favors the status quo, and the role of compromise and negotiation in lawmaking. Vocabulary: legislation, bill, committee, markup, floor vote, filibuster, cloture, conference committee, veto, override, revenue bill, Speaker.

This topic covers Executive Power for Constitution 101 students (Const 101: Article II). Students should understand the constitutional powers and practical authority of the presidency. Key concepts: Article II vests executive power in the President; constitutional powers include serving as commander-in-chief, making treaties (with Senate consent), appointing judges and officials (with Senate confirmation), granting pardons, delivering the State of the Union, and faithfully executing the laws; informal powers have expanded presidential authority beyond the text: executive orders direct federal agencies, executive agreements bypass treaty ratification, signing statements express interpretations of legislation, and the bully pulpit shapes public opinion; the growth of presidential power from a limited to a dominant role reflects crises (wars, depressions), media, and public expectations. Students should analyze the tension between presidential leadership and constitutional limits and evaluate whether the modern presidency has exceeded its constitutional bounds. Vocabulary: executive power, commander-in-chief, executive order, treaty, appointment, pardon, bully pulpit, signing statement, Article II.

Constitution 101.ref: Const 101: Bill of Rights and 14th Amendment
Constitution 101.prompt: This topic covers Constitutional Rights for Constitution 101 students (Const 101: Bill of Rights and 14th Amendment). Students should understand how constitutional rights have been defined, expanded, and debated throughout American history. Key concepts: rights in the Constitution come from the Bill of Rights (first 10 amendments), subsequent amendments (especially the 14th), and judicial interpretation; the 14th Amendment's Due Process and Equal Protection Clauses have been the basis for expanding rights to groups originally excluded; the incorporation doctrine applied most Bill of Rights protections to state governments; unenumerated rights (privacy, travel, vote, parental rights) have been recognized through the 9th Amendment and substantive due process; rights are not absolute and can be limited when compelling government interests exist (balancing tests, strict scrutiny, intermediate scrutiny, rational basis review). Students should understand how the scope of constitutional rights changes over time through judicial interpretation and amendment. Vocabulary: constitutional rights, due process, equal protection, incorporation, unenumerated rights, strict scrutiny, compelling interest, balancing test, 14th Amendment.

This topic covers the Constitution in Modern Society for Constitution 101 students (Const 101: Contemporary Constitutional Issues). Students should understand how the 18th-century Constitution applies to 21st-century challenges. Key concepts: digital privacy and the Fourth Amendment (do police need warrants to search cell phones, track GPS locations, access email?); free speech in the age of social media (does the First Amendment apply to private platforms?); executive power in the War on Terror (detention, surveillance, drone strikes); Second Amendment debates (individual right vs. well-regulated militia, gun regulation); equal protection and evolving conceptions of equality (same-sex marriage, gender identity, affirmative action); federalism tensions (immigration, marijuana legalization, healthcare); the role of the Electoral College in modern elections. Students should analyze how constitutional principles apply to contemporary issues and understand that the Constitution's meaning continues to evolve through interpretation and practice. Vocabulary: digital privacy, social media, surveillance, gun rights, marriage equality, immigration, Electoral College, executive power, constitutional debate.

This topic covers the Constitution and Democracy for Constitution 101 students (Const 101: Democratic Principles and Republicanism). Students should understand the relationship between the Constitution and democratic governance. Key concepts: the Constitution establishes a republic (representative government) rather than a direct democracy; the Framers deliberately included both democratic and counter-majoritarian elements; democratic features include elected representatives, popular sovereignty, and the amendment process; counter-majoritarian features include the Senate (equal state representation), the Electoral College, judicial review (unelected judges overriding majority will), the Bill of Rights (protecting minorities from majority tyranny), and the difficult amendment process. Students should analyze the tension between majority rule and minority rights, understand why the Framers feared 'tyranny of the majority' (Federalist No. 10), and evaluate whether the Constitution's counter-majoritarian features strengthen or weaken democracy. Vocabulary: democracy, republic, majority rule, minority rights, popular sovereignty, representation, counter-majoritarian, tyranny of the majority, Electoral College.

This topic covers the Future of the Constitution for Constitution 101 students (Const 101: Constitutional Evolution and Amendment). Students should understand ongoing debates about how the Constitution should adapt to changing circumstances. Key concepts: the Constitution has endured for over 230 years, making it the oldest written national constitution still in use; proposed amendments that have not been ratified (Equal Rights Amendment, balanced budget amendment, term limits for Congress); the debate over calling a constitutional convention under Article V (never used, feared as potentially runaway); informal constitutional change through judicial interpretation, legislative practice, executive action, and custom; arguments for a new constitutional convention (outdated provisions, undemocratic features) vs. arguments against (risk of radical change, difficulty of reaching consensus); comparative perspectives (most countries have rewritten their constitutions). Students should evaluate whether the Constitution remains adequate for modern challenges and what changes, if any, might be needed. Vocabulary: constitutional evolution, Article V convention, informal amendment, ERA, term limits, convention of states, living document, constitutional reform, durability.
